notes.note: The rustic festival of Pales (21 April), also Rome's birthday; Cicero is needling Atticus over the news that his nephew young Quintus was crowned (publicly honoured) at the celebration in Antony's company.
A Roman date reckoned by counting back inclusively from the Kalends (first) of the following month; here the sixth day before the Kalends of May, i.e. 26 April 44 BC.
A mock-philosophical "school" (haeresis) named for Vestorius, a Puteoli banker known for his peculiar arithmetic and businessman's jargon; Atticus had evidently parodied his manner.
A textual crux: the Latin (†Pherionum more†) is corrupt, marked by the editors' daggers, so the joke Atticus made about some "Pherionian" manner at Puteoli cannot be recovered. Flag-not-resolve.
Rufio, an agent or freedman attached to Vestorius's circle, named as a coming nobody Cicero now expects to have to obey; he cannot be securely identified with any registered Rufio. Flag-not-resolve.
The festival of Liber (17 March); the Senate met that day in 44 BC, two days after the assassination, the occasion of the strained debate Cicero recalls.
After killing Caesar the conspirators withdrew to the Capitol and held council there; Cicero notes that he had disapproved of this passive occupation rather than decisive action.

A pun: Cicero turns the cognomen Brutus (which as a common noun means "dull, stupid") against the timid senators who merely applauded the deed without seeing it through. The wordplay does not name the Liberators.
The assassination of Caesar on 15 March 44 BC; Cicero's bitter point is that the deed opened heaven to its doers but failed to restore the Republic.
Caesar's funeral, at which Antony's eulogy and the display of the bloodied corpse roused the mob against the Liberators; Atticus had foreseen the danger of allowing it.
The first of June (1 June 44 BC), the date set for Antony to bring the provincial commands before the Senate; a calendar date, not a person.
Cisalpine and Transalpine Gaul; Antony was manoeuvring to have both provinces assigned to himself with extended terms, the move that precipitated the war with Decimus Brutus.

notes.anchor: depredations are going on at the Temple of Ops
notes.note: After the Ides, Antony seized the public treasury of some 700 million sesterces that Caesar had stored in the temple of Ops on the Capitol; no temple-of-Ops entity is registered. Flag-not-resolve.
Buthrotum in Epirus, where Atticus held large estates; the town faced confiscation under a Caesarian colony scheme, and Cicero promises to push a favourable Senate decree on its behalf.
The Greek city of Massilia (Marseilles), punished by Caesar for siding with Pompey; Cicero teases Atticus for already "restoring" their lost territory as though the Republic were back.
